label: Contributory Negligence
scenario_patterns: A jaywalking pedestrian is hit by a speeding driver
A motorcyclist not wearing a helmet suffers head injuries in a collision caused by another driver
A worker ignores safety protocols and is injured by machinery negligently maintained by the employer
A patient fails to follow medical advice, worsening injuries caused by a doctor's initial negligence
legal_tests: The plaintiff failed to take reasonable care for their own safety
The plaintiff's failure to take care materially contributed to the damage suffered
Apportionment is governed by the Contributory Negligence and Personal Injuries Act (Cap 54)
The court reduces damages by a percentage that is just and equitable having regard to the plaintiff's share of responsibility
Both causative potency and blameworthiness of each party's conduct are considered in apportionment
common_fact_patterns: Pedestrians jaywalking or crossing roads without due care in Singapore
Failure to wear seatbelts or motorcycle helmets, increasing severity of injuries
Workers disregarding workplace safety instructions or not using personal protective equipment
Passengers in vehicles driven by intoxicated drivers whom they knew to be drunk
Plaintiffs failing to mitigate injuries by not following medical treatment advice
sg_case_references: Asnah bte Ab Rahman v Li Jianlin [2016] SGCA 16 — apportionment between jaywalking pedestrian and negligent driver
Ng Swee Eng v Ang Oh Chuan [2002] 2 SLR(R) 321 — contributory negligence of motorcycle pillion rider not wearing helmet
Parno v SC Marine Pte Ltd [1999] SGCA 69 — contributory negligence of a worker in a workplace accident
Tan Hun Hoe v Harte Denis Mathew [2001] SGCA 48 — apportionment in medical negligence context
defences: This is itself a defence — contributory negligence is raised by the defendant to reduce the plaintiff's damages
The plaintiff's conduct was not merely careless but the sole effective cause of the damage (100% contributory negligence amounting to a complete defence)
Apportionment may also be raised between concurrent tortfeasors under the Civil Law Act (Cap 43) for contribution
